explicit_text: I was born and raised in Laos, where my educational journey began under challenging circumstances. From a young age, I had to live away from my family to attend school in different villages and towns across the country. Despite the hardships and being separated from my parents, I worked hard and eventually earned a degree in physician assistance in Vientiane, the capital of Laos.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in a Southeast Asian country known for its mountainous terrain and rich cultural heritage, where my educational journey began under challenging circumstances. From a young age, I had to live away from my family to attend school in different villages and towns across the nation. Despite the hardships and being separated from my parents, I worked hard and eventually earned a degree in physician assistance in the capital city.

explicit_text: When I was 12, I moved to Muang Cha to attend elementary school, living with my uncle about 50 miles away from my family. It took me two and a half days to walk there, and I had to balance school with daily chores like feeding pigs and chickens. This was my first experience living independently, and although I was excited to go to school, I only saw my family a few times a year.
implicit_text: When I was 12, I moved to a small town about 50 miles away from my family to attend elementary school, living with my uncle. It took me two and a half days to walk there, and I had to balance school with daily chores like feeding pigs and chickens. This was my first experience living independently, and although I was excited to go to school, I only saw my family a few times a year.

explicit_text: After elementary school, I moved even further to Phon Hong for middle school, which was 250 kilometers away. I was fortunate to ride an old truck used by soldiers during the Vietnam War to get there. In Phon Hong, I lived with my uncle on my mother's side and helped with farming on weekends while attending school during the week, though I only saw my family once a year.
implicit_text: After elementary school, I moved even further to a district located 250 kilometers away for middle school. I was fortunate to ride an old truck used by soldiers during the Vietnam War to get there. In this new place, I lived with my uncle on my mother's side and helped with farming on weekends while attending school during the week, though I only saw my family once a year.

explicit_text: I eventually made my way to Vientiane, the capital city of Laos, to attend college where I earned a degree in physician assistance at age 26. I received a scholarship and lived in a dormitory, supporting myself with part-time jobs like delivering newspapers and volunteering at the university library. This was a pivotal time in my life, though my parents had already left Laos for the United States by then.

I received a scholarship and lived in a dormitory, supporting myself with part-time jobs like delivering newspapers and volunteering at the university library. explicit_text: In 2007, I moved to St. Paul, Minnesota to reunite with my family after many years apart. It was a bittersweet transition because although I was finally close to my loved ones, I had to start my education over since my degree from Laos was not recognized here. Learning a new language and adapting to a new system has been difficult, but I am determined to succeed.

explicit_text: My father worked for the Central Intelligence Agency during the Vietnam War, which greatly affected our family life. Because of his work, he had to leave Laos when I was in middle school, and later my mother also moved to the United States. Their departure meant I had to live on my own for many years while pursuing my education in Laos.

explicit_text: The Vietnam War had a significant impact on my life and family, especially since my father was involved with the CIA during that time. The war influenced the transportation I used to get to school, like the old soldier's truck in Phon Hong. It also led to my family leaving Laos for the United States, which shaped my educational and personal journey.

explicit_text: Currently, I am studying at the Hubbs Center here in the United States to continue my education. This time, my experience is different because I am finally close to my family, which gives me more support. I am working hard to improve my English and earn a degree that will allow me to use my knowledge professionally in America.

The transition has been challenging, but being reunited with my family and studying at the Hubbs Center gives me strength to pursue my goals.
